NKJV.1: Now it happened in Iconium that they went together to the synagogue of the Jews, and so spoke that a great multitude both of the Jews and of the Greeks believed.
NKJV.2: But the unbelieving Jews stirred up the Gentiles and poisoned their minds against the brethren.
NKJV.3: Therefore they stayed there a long time, speaking boldly in the Lord, who was bearing witness to the word of His grace, granting signs and wonders to be done by their hands.
NKJV.4: But the multitude of the city was divided: part sided with the Jews, and part with the apostles.
NKJV.5: And when a violent attempt was made by both the Gentiles and Jews, with their rulers, to abuse and stone them,
NKJV.6: they became aware of it and fled to Lystra and Derbe, cities of Lycaonia, and to the surrounding region.
NKJV.7: And they were preaching the gospel there.
NKJV.8: And in Lystra a certain man without strength in his feet was sitting, a cripple from his mother’s womb, who had never walked.
NKJV.9: This man heard Paul speaking. Paul, observing him intently and seeing that he had faith to be healed,
NKJV.10: said with a loud voice, “Stand up straight on your feet!” And he leaped and walked.
NKJV.11: Now when the people saw what Paul had done, they raised their voices, saying in the Lycaonian language, “The gods have come down to us in the likeness of men!”
NKJV.12: And Barnabas they called Zeus, and Paul, Hermes, because he was the chief speaker.
NKJV.13: Then the priest of Zeus, whose temple was in front of their city, brought oxen and garlands to the gates, intending to sacrifice with the multitudes.
NKJV.14: But when the apostles Barnabas and Paul heard this, they tore their clothes and ran in among the multitude, crying out
NKJV.15: and saying, “Men, why are you doing these things? We also are men with the same nature as you, and preach to you that you should turn from these useless things to the living God, who made the heaven, the earth, the sea, and all things that are in them,
NKJV.16: who in bygone generations allowed all nations to walk in their own ways.
NKJV.17: Nevertheless He did not leave Himself without witness, in that He did good, gave us rain from heaven and fruitful seasons, filling our hearts with food and gladness.”
NKJV.18: And with these sayings they could scarcely restrain the multitudes from sacrificing to them.
NKJV.19: Then Jews from Antioch and Iconium came there; and having persuaded the multitudes, they stoned Paul and dragged him out of the city, supposing him to be dead.
NKJV.20: However, when the disciples gathered around him, he rose up and went into the city. And the next day he departed with Barnabas to Derbe.
NKJV.21: And when they had preached the gospel to that city and made many disciples, they returned to Lystra, Iconium, and Antioch,
NKJV.22: strengthening the souls of the disciples, exhorting them to continue in the faith, and saying, “We must through many tribulations enter the kingdom of God.”
NKJV.23: So when they had appointed elders in every church, and prayed with fasting, they commended them to the Lord in whom they had believed.
NKJV.24: And after they had passed through Pisidia, they came to Pamphylia.
NKJV.25: Now when they had preached the word in Perga, they went down to Attalia.
NKJV.26: From there they sailed to Antioch, where they had been commended to the grace of God for the work which they had completed.
NKJV.27: Now when they had come and gathered the church together, they reported all that God had done with them, and that He had opened the door of faith to the Gentiles.
NKJV.28: So they stayed there a long time with the disciples.

ESV.1: Now at Iconium they entered together into the Jewish synagogue and spoke in such a way that a great number of both Jews and Greeks believed.
ESV.2: But the unbelieving Jews stirred up the Gentiles and poisoned their minds against the brothers.
ESV.3: So they remained for a long time, speaking boldly for the Lord, who bore witness to the word of his grace, granting signs and wonders to be done by their hands.
ESV.4: But the people of the city were divided; some sided with the Jews and some with the apostles.
ESV.5: When an attempt was made by both Gentiles and Jews, with their rulers, to mistreat them and to stone them,
ESV.6: they learned of it and fled to Lystra and Derbe, cities of Lycaonia, and to the surrounding country,
ESV.7: and there they continued to preach the gospel.
ESV.8: Now at Lystra there was a man sitting who could not use his feet. He was crippled from birth and had never walked.
ESV.9: He listened to Paul speaking. And Paul, looking intently at him and seeing that he had faith to be made well,
ESV.10: said in a loud voice, “Stand upright on your feet.” And he sprang up and began walking.
ESV.11: And when the crowds saw what Paul had done, they lifted up their voices, saying in Lycaonian, “The gods have come down to us in the likeness of men!”
ESV.12: Barnabas they called Zeus, and Paul, Hermes, because he was the chief speaker.
ESV.13: And the priest of Zeus, whose temple was at the entrance to the city, brought oxen and garlands to the gates and wanted to offer sacrifice with the crowds.
ESV.14: But when the apostles Barnabas and Paul heard of it, they tore their garments and rushed out into the crowd, crying out,
ESV.15: “Men, why are you doing these things? We also are men, of like nature with you, and we bring you good news, that you should turn from these vain things to a living God, who made the heaven and the earth and the sea and all that is in them.
ESV.16: In past generations he allowed all the nations to walk in their own ways.
ESV.17: Yet he did not leave himself without witness, for he did good by giving you rains from heaven and fruitful seasons, satisfying your hearts with food and gladness.”
ESV.18: Even with these words they scarcely restrained the people from offering sacrifice to them.
ESV.19: But Jews came from Antioch and Iconium, and having persuaded the crowds, they stoned Paul and dragged him out of the city, supposing that he was dead.
ESV.20: But when the disciples gathered about him, he rose up and entered the city, and on the next day he went on with Barnabas to Derbe.
ESV.21: When they had preached the gospel to that city and had made many disciples, they returned to Lystra and to Iconium and to Antioch,
ESV.22: strengthening the souls of the disciples, encouraging them to continue in the faith, and saying that through many tribulations we must enter the kingdom of God.
ESV.23: And when they had appointed elders for them in every church, with prayer and fasting they committed them to the Lord in whom they had believed.
ESV.24: Then they passed through Pisidia and came to Pamphylia.
ESV.25: And when they had spoken the word in Perga, they went down to Attalia,
ESV.26: and from there they sailed to Antioch, where they had been commended to the grace of God for the work that they had fulfilled.
ESV.27: And when they arrived and gathered the church together, they declared all that God had done with them, and how he had opened a door of faith to the Gentiles.
ESV.28: And they remained no little time with the disciples.

NASB.1: In Iconium they entered the synagogue of the Jews together, and spoke in such a manner that a large number of people believed, both of Jews and of Greeks.
NASB.2: But the Jews who disbelieved stirred up the minds of the Gentiles and embittered them against the brethren.
NASB.3: Therefore they spent a long time [there] speaking boldly [with reliance] upon the Lord, who was testifying to the word of His grace, granting that signs and wonders be done by their hands.
NASB.4: But the people of the city were divided; and some sided with the Jews, and some with the apostles.
NASB.5: And when an attempt was made by both the Gentiles and the Jews with their rulers, to mistreat and to stone them,
NASB.6: they became aware of it and fled to the cities of Lycaonia, Lystra and Derbe, and the surrounding region;
NASB.7: and there they continued to preach the gospel.
NASB.8: At Lystra a man was sitting who had no strength in his feet, lame from his mother's womb, who had never walked.
NASB.9: This man was listening to Paul as he spoke, who, when he had fixed his gaze on him and had seen that he had faith to be made well,
NASB.10: said with a loud voice, 'Stand upright on your feet.' And he leaped up and [began] to walk.
NASB.11: When the crowds saw what Paul had done, they raised their voice, saying in the Lycaonian language, 'The gods have become like men and have come down to us.'
NASB.12: And they [began] calling Barnabas, Zeus, and Paul, Hermes, because he was the chief speaker.
NASB.13: The priest of Zeus, whose [temple] was just outside the city, brought oxen and garlands to the gates, and wanted to offer sacrifice with the crowds.
NASB.14: But when the apostles Barnabas and Paul heard of it, they tore their robes and rushed out into the crowd, crying out
NASB.15: and saying, 'Men, why are you doing these things? We are also men of the same nature as you, and preach the gospel to you that you should turn from these vain things to a living God, WHO MADE THE HEAVEN AND THE EARTH AND THE SEA AND ALL THAT IS IN THEM.
NASB.16: 'In the generations gone by He permitted all the nations to go their own ways;
NASB.17: and yet He did not leave Himself without witness, in that He did good and gave you rains from heaven and fruitful seasons, satisfying your hearts with food and gladness.'
NASB.18: [Even] saying these things, with difficulty they restrained the crowds from offering sacrifice to them.
NASB.19: But Jews came from Antioch and Iconium, and having won over the crowds, they stoned Paul and dragged him out of the city, supposing him to be dead.
NASB.20: But while the disciples stood around him, he got up and entered the city. The next day he went away with Barnabas to Derbe.
NASB.21: After they had preached the gospel to that city and had made many disciples, they returned to Lystra and to Iconium and to Antioch,
NASB.22: strengthening the souls of the disciples, encouraging them to continue in the faith, and [saying], 'Through many tribulations we must enter the kingdom of God.'
NASB.23: When they had appointed elders for them in every church, having prayed with fasting, they commended them to the Lord in whom they had believed.
NASB.24: They passed through Pisidia and came into Pamphylia.
NASB.25: When they had spoken the word in Perga, they went down to Attalia.
NASB.26: From there they sailed to Antioch, from which they had been commended to the grace of God for the work that they had accomplished.
NASB.27: When they had arrived and gathered the church together, they [began] to report all things that God had done with them and how He had opened a door of faith to the Gentiles.
NASB.28: And they spent a long time with the disciples.

NLT.1: The same thing happened in Iconium. Paul and Barnabas went to the Jewish synagogue and preached with such power that a great number of both Jews and Greeks became believers.
NLT.2: Some of the Jews, however, spurned God’s message and poisoned the minds of the Gentiles against Paul and Barnabas.
NLT.3: But the apostles stayed there a long time, preaching boldly about the grace of the Lord. And the Lord proved their message was true by giving them power to do miraculous signs and wonders.
NLT.4: But the people of the town were divided in their opinion about them. Some sided with the Jews, and some with the apostles.
NLT.5: Then a mob of Gentiles and Jews, along with their leaders, decided to attack and stone them.
NLT.6: When the apostles learned of it, they fled to the region of Lycaonia — to the towns of Lystra and Derbe and the surrounding area.
NLT.7: And there they preached the Good News.
NLT.8: While they were at Lystra, Paul and Barnabas came upon a man with crippled feet. He had been that way from birth, so he had never walked. He was sitting
NLT.9: and listening as Paul preached. Looking straight at him, Paul realized he had faith to be healed.
NLT.10: So Paul called to him in a loud voice, “Stand up!” And the man jumped to his feet and started walking.
NLT.11: When the crowd saw what Paul had done, they shouted in their local dialect, “These men are gods in human form!”
NLT.12: They decided that Barnabas was the Greek god Zeus and that Paul was Hermes, since he was the chief speaker.
NLT.13: Now the temple of Zeus was located just outside the town. So the priest of the temple and the crowd brought bulls and wreaths of flowers to the town gates, and they prepared to offer sacrifices to the apostles.
NLT.14: But when the apostles Barnabas and Paul heard what was happening, they tore their clothing in dismay and ran out among the people, shouting,
NLT.15: “Friends, why are you doing this? We are merely human beings — just like you! We have come to bring you the Good News that you should turn from these worthless things and turn to the living God, who made heaven and earth, the sea, and everything in them.
NLT.16: In the past he permitted all the nations to go their own ways,
NLT.17: but he never left them without evidence of himself and his goodness. For instance, he sends you rain and good crops and gives you food and joyful hearts.”
NLT.18: But even with these words, Paul and Barnabas could scarcely restrain the people from sacrificing to them.
NLT.19: Then some Jews arrived from Antioch and Iconium and won the crowds to their side. They stoned Paul and dragged him out of town, thinking he was dead.
NLT.20: But as the believers gathered around him, he got up and went back into the town. The next day he left with Barnabas for Derbe.
NLT.21: After preaching the Good News in Derbe and making many disciples, Paul and Barnabas returned to Lystra, Iconium, and Antioch of Pisidia,
NLT.22: where they strengthened the believers. They encouraged them to continue in the faith, reminding them that we must suffer many hardships to enter the Kingdom of God.
NLT.23: Paul and Barnabas also appointed elders in every church. With prayer and fasting, they turned the elders over to the care of the Lord, in whom they had put their trust.
NLT.24: Then they traveled back through Pisidia to Pamphylia.
NLT.25: They preached the word in Perga, then went down to Attalia.
NLT.26: Finally, they returned by ship to Antioch of Syria, where their journey had begun. The believers there had entrusted them to the grace of God to do the work they had now completed.
NLT.27: Upon arriving in Antioch, they called the church together and reported everything God had done through them and how he had opened the door of faith to the Gentiles, too.
NLT.28: And they stayed there with the believers for a long time.

WEB.1: In Iconium, they entered together into the synagogue of the Jews, and so spoke that a great multitude both of Jews and of Greeks believed.
WEB.2: But the disbelieving  Jews stirred up and embittered the souls of the Gentiles against the brothers.
WEB.3: Therefore they stayed there a long time, speaking boldly in the Lord, who testified to the word of his grace, granting signs and wonders to be done by their hands.
WEB.4: But the multitude of the city was divided. Part sided with the Jews, and part with the apostles.
WEB.5: When some of both the Gentiles and the Jews, with their rulers, made a violent attempt to mistreat and stone them,
WEB.6: they became aware of it, and fled to the cities of Lycaonia, Lystra, Derbe, and the surrounding region.
WEB.7: There they preached the Good News.
WEB.8: At Lystra a certain man sat, impotent in his feet, a cripple from his mother’s womb, who never had walked.
WEB.9: He was listening to Paul speaking, who, fastening eyes on him, and seeing that he had faith to be made whole,
WEB.10: said with a loud voice, “Stand upright on your feet!” He leaped up and walked.
WEB.11: When the multitude saw what Paul had done, they lifted up their voice, saying in the language of Lycaonia, “The gods have come down to us in the likeness of men!”
WEB.12: They called Barnabas “Jupiter”, and Paul “Mercury”, because he was the chief speaker.
WEB.13: The priest of Jupiter, whose temple was in front of their city, brought oxen and garlands to the gates, and would have made a sacrifice along with the multitudes.
WEB.14: But when the apostles, Barnabas and Paul, heard of it, they tore their clothes, and sprang into the multitude, crying out,
WEB.15: “Men, why are you doing these things? We also are men of like passions with you, and bring you good news, that you should turn from these vain things to the living God, who made the sky, the earth, the sea, and all that is in them;
WEB.16: who in the generations gone by allowed all the nations to walk in their own ways.
WEB.17: Yet he didn’t leave himself without witness, in that he did good and gave you  rains from the sky and fruitful seasons, filling our hearts with food and gladness.”
WEB.18: Even saying these things, they hardly stopped the multitudes from making a sacrifice to them.
WEB.19: But some Jews from Antioch and Iconium came there, and having persuaded the multitudes, they stoned Paul, and dragged him out of the city, supposing that he was dead.
WEB.20: But as the disciples stood around him, he rose up, and entered into the city. On the next day he went out with Barnabas to Derbe.
WEB.21: When they had preached the Good News to that city, and had made many disciples, they returned to Lystra, Iconium, and Antioch,
WEB.22: confirming the souls of the disciples, exhorting them to continue in the faith, and that through many afflictions we must enter into God’s Kingdom.
WEB.23: When they had appointed elders for them in every assembly, and had prayed with fasting, they commended them to the Lord, on whom they had believed.
WEB.24: They passed through Pisidia, and came to Pamphylia.
WEB.25: When they had spoken the word in Perga, they went down to Attalia.
WEB.26: From there they sailed to Antioch, from where they had been committed to the grace of God for the work which they had fulfilled.
WEB.27: When they had arrived, and had gathered the assembly together, they reported all the things that God had done with them, and that he had opened a door of faith to the nations.
WEB.28: They stayed there with the disciples for a long time.

CSB17.1: In Iconium they entered the Jewish synagogue, as usual, and spoke in such a way that a great number of both Jews and Greeks believed. <sup>ⓔ</sup>
CSB17.2: But the unbelieving Jews stirred up the Gentiles and poisoned their minds against the brothers. <sup>ⓕ</sup>
CSB17.3: So they stayed there a long time and spoke boldly for the Lord, who testified to the message of his grace by enabling them to do signs and wonders. <sup>ⓖ</sup>
CSB17.4: But the people of the city were divided, some siding with the Jews and others with the apostles. <sup>ⓗ</sup>
CSB17.5: When an attempt was made by both the Gentiles and Jews, with their rulers, to mistreat and stone them,
CSB17.6: they found out about it and fled to the Lycaonian towns of Lystra and Derbe and to the surrounding countryside. <sup>ⓘ</sup>
CSB17.7: There they continued preaching the gospel. <sup>ⓙ</sup>
CSB17.8: In Lystra a man was sitting who was without strength in his feet, had never walked, and had been lame from birth.
CSB17.9: He listened as Paul spoke. After looking directly at him and seeing that he had faith to be healed,
CSB17.10: Paul said in a loud voice, “Stand up on your feet!” And he jumped up and began to walk around. <sup>ⓚ</sup>
CSB17.11: When the crowds saw what Paul had done, they shouted, saying in the Lycaonian language, “The gods have come down to us in human form!” <sup>ⓛ</sup>
CSB17.12: Barnabas they called Zeus, and Paul, Hermes, because he was the chief speaker.
CSB17.13: The priest of Zeus, whose temple was just outside the town, brought bulls and wreaths to the gates because he intended, with the crowds, to offer sacrifice.
CSB17.14: The apostles Barnabas and Paul tore their robes when they heard this and rushed into the crowd, shouting: <sup>ⓜ</sup>
CSB17.15: “People! Why are you doing these things? We are people also, just like you, and we are proclaiming good news to you, that you turn from these worthless things ⓝ to the living God, who made the heaven, the earth, the sea, and everything in them. [1] ⓐ
CSB17.16: In past generations he allowed all the nations <sup>ⓑ</sup> to go their own way,
CSB17.17: although he did not leave himself without a witness, <sup>ⓒ</sup> since he did what is good by giving you rain from heaven and fruitful seasons <sup>ⓓ</sup> and filling you with food and your <sup>[2]</sup> hearts with joy.”
CSB17.18: Even though they said these things, they barely stopped the crowds from sacrificing to them.
CSB17.19: Some Jews came from Antioch and Iconium, and when they won over the crowds, they stoned Paul and dragged him out of the city, thinking he was dead. <sup>ⓔ</sup>
CSB17.20: After the disciples gathered around him, he got up and went into the town. The next day he left with Barnabas for Derbe. <sup>ⓕ</sup>
CSB17.21: After they had preached the gospel in that town and made many disciples, they returned to Lystra, to Iconium, and to Antioch, <sup>ⓖ</sup>
CSB17.22: strengthening the <sup>[3]</sup> disciples by encouraging them to continue in the faith <sup>ⓗ</sup> and by telling them, “It is necessary to go through many hardships <sup>ⓘ</sup> to enter the kingdom of God.”
CSB17.23: When they had appointed elders <sup>ⓙ</sup> for them in every church and prayed with fasting, they committed them to the Lord in whom they had believed.
CSB17.24: They passed through Pisidia and came to Pamphylia.
CSB17.25: After they had spoken the word in Perga, they went down to Attalia.
CSB17.26: From there they sailed back to Antioch where they had been commended to the grace of God for the work they had now completed. <sup>ⓚ</sup>
CSB17.27: After they arrived and gathered the church together, they reported everything God had done with them <sup>ⓛ</sup> and that he had opened the door <sup>ⓜ</sup> of faith to the Gentiles.
CSB17.28: And they spent a considerable time with the disciples.

AMP.1: Now in Iconium Paul and Barnabas went into the Jewish synagogue together and spoke in such a way [with such power and boldness] that a large number of Jews as well as Greeks believed [and confidently accepted Jesus as Savior];
AMP.2: but the unbelieving Jews [who rejected Jesus as Messiah] stirred up and embittered the minds of the Gentiles against the believers.
AMP.3: So Paul and Barnabas stayed for a long time, speaking boldly and confidently for the Lord, who continued to testify to the word of His grace, granting that signs and wonders (attesting miracles) be done by them.
AMP.4: But the people of the city were divided; some were siding with the Jews, and some with the apostles.
AMP.5: When there was an attempt by both the Gentiles and the Jews, together with their rulers, to shamefully mistreat and to stone them,
AMP.6: they, aware of the situation, escaped to Lystra and Derbe, [taking refuge in the] cities of Lycaonia, and the neighboring region;
AMP.7: and there they continued to preach the good news.
AMP.8: Now at Lystra a man sat who was unable to use his feet, for he was crippled from birth and had never walked.
AMP.9: This man was listening to Paul as he spoke, and Paul looked intently at him and saw that he had faith to be healed,
AMP.10: and said with a loud voice, "Stand up on your feet." And he jumped up and began to walk.
AMP.11: And the crowds, when they saw what Paul had done, raised their voices, shouting in the Lycaonian language, "The gods have come down to us in human form!"
AMP.12: They began calling Barnabas, Zeus [chief of the Greek gods], and Paul, Hermes [messenger of the Greek gods], since he took the lead in speaking.
AMP.13: The priest of Zeus, whose temple was at the entrance of the city, brought bulls and garlands to the city gates, and wanted to offer sacrifices with the crowds.
AMP.14: But when the apostles Barnabas and Paul heard about it, they tore their robes and rushed out into the crowd, shouting,
AMP.15: "Men, why are you doing these things? We too are only men of the same nature as you, bringing the good news to you, so that you turn from these useless and meaningless things to the living God, who made the heaven and the earth and the sea and everything that is in them.
AMP.16: In generations past He permitted all the nations to go their own ways;
AMP.17: yet He did not leave Himself without some witness [as evidence of Himself], in that He kept constantly doing good things and showing you kindness, and giving you rains from heaven and productive seasons, filling your hearts with food and happiness."
AMP.18: Even saying these words, with difficulty they prevented the people from offering sacrifices to them.
AMP.19: But Jews arrived from Antioch and Iconium, and having won over the crowds, they stoned Paul and dragged him out of the city, thinking he was dead.
AMP.20: But the disciples formed a circle around him, and he got up and went back into the city; and the next day he went on with Barnabas to Derbe.
AMP.21: They preached the good news to that city and made many disciples, then they returned to Lystra and to Iconium and to Antioch,
AMP.22: strengthening and establishing the hearts of the disciples; encouraging them to remain firm in the faith, saying, "It is through many tribulations and hardships that we must enter the kingdom of God."
AMP.23: When they had appointed elders for them in every church, having prayed with fasting, they entrusted them to the Lord in whom they believed [and joyfully accepted as the Messiah].
AMP.24: Then they passed through Pisidia and came to Pamphylia.
AMP.25: When they had spoken the word [of salvation through faith in Christ] in Perga, they went down to Attalia.
AMP.26: From there they sailed back to Antioch, where they had been entrusted to the grace of God for the work which they had now completed.
AMP.27: Arriving there, they gathered the church together and began to report [in great detail] everything that God had done with them and how He had opened to the Gentiles a door of faith [in Jesus as the Messiah and Savior].
AMP.28: And they stayed there a long time with the disciples.

MSG.1: When they got to Iconium they went, as they always did, to the meeting place of the Jews and gave their message. The Message convinced both Jews and non-Jews—and not just a few, either.
MSG.2: But the unbelieving Jews worked up a whispering campaign against Paul and Barnabas, sowing mistrust and suspicion in the minds of the people in the street.
MSG.3: The two apostles were there a long time, speaking freely, openly, and confidently as they presented the clear evidence of God's gifts, God corroborating their work with miracles and wonders.
MSG.4: But then there was a split in public opinion, some siding with the Jews, some with the apostles.
MSG.5: One day, learning that both the Jews and non-Jews had been organized by their leaders to beat them up,
MSG.6: they escaped as best they could to the next towns—Lyconia, Lystra, Derbe, and that neighborhood—
MSG.7: but then were right back at it again, getting out the Message.
MSG.8: There was a man in Lystra who couldn't walk. He sat there, crippled since the day of his birth.
MSG.9: He heard Paul talking, and Paul, looking him in the eye, saw that he was ripe for God's work, ready to believe.
MSG.10: So he said, loud enough for everyone to hear, "Up on your feet!" The man was up in a flash—jumped up and walked around as if he'd been walking all his life.
MSG.11: When the crowd saw what Paul had done, they went wild, calling out in their Lyconian dialect, "The gods have come down! These men are gods!"
MSG.12: They called Barnabas "Zeus" and Paul "Hermes" (since Paul did most of the speaking).
MSG.13: The priest of the local Zeus shrine got up a parade—bulls and banners and people lined right up to the gates, ready for the ritual of sacrifice.
MSG.14: When Barnabas and Paul finally realized what was going on, they stopped them. Waving their arms, they interrupted the parade, calling out,
MSG.15: "What do you think you're doing! We're not gods! We are men just like you, and we're here to bring you the Message, to persuade you to abandon these silly god-superstitions and embrace God himself, the living God. We don't make God; he makes us, and all of this—sky, earth, sea, and everything in them.
MSG.16: "In the generations before us, God let all the different nations go their own way.
MSG.17: But even then he didn't leave them without a clue, for he made a good creation, poured down rain and gave bumper crops. When your bellies were full and your hearts happy, there was evidence of good beyond your doing."
MSG.18: Talking fast and hard like this, they prevented them from carrying out the sacrifice that would have honored them as gods—but just barely.
MSG.19: Then some Jews from Antioch and Iconium caught up with them and turned the fickle crowd against them. They beat Paul unconscious, dragged him outside the town and left him for dead.
MSG.20: But as the disciples gathered around him, he came to and got up. He went back into town and the next day left with Barnabas for Derbe.
MSG.21: After proclaiming the Message in Derbe and establishing a strong core of disciples, they retraced their steps to Lystra, then Iconium, and then Antioch,
MSG.22: putting muscle and sinew in the lives of the disciples, urging them to stick with what they had begun to believe and not quit, making it clear to them that it wouldn't be easy: "Anyone signing up for the kingdom of God has to go through plenty of hard times."
MSG.23: Paul and Barnabas handpicked leaders in each church. After praying—their prayers intensified by fasting—they presented these new leaders to the Master to whom they had entrusted their lives.
MSG.24: Working their way back through Pisidia, they came to Pamphylia
MSG.25: and preached in Perga. Finally, they made it to Attalia
MSG.26: and caught a ship back to Antioch, where it had all started—launched by God's grace and now safely home by God's grace. A good piece of work.
MSG.27: On arrival, they got the church together and reported on their trip, telling in detail how God had used them to throw the door of faith wide open so people of all nations could come streaming in.
MSG.28: Then they settled down for a long, leisurely visit with the disciples.

NIV.1:
In Iconium
At Iconium Paul and Barnabas went as usual into the Jewish synagogue. There they spoke so effectively that a great number of Jews and Gentiles believed.
NIV.2: But the Jews who refused to believe stirred up the Gentiles and poisoned their minds against the brothers.
NIV.3: So Paul and Barnabas spent considerable time there, speaking boldly for the Lord, who confirmed the message of his grace by enabling them to do miraculous signs and wonders.
NIV.4: The people of the city were divided; some sided with the Jews, others with the apostles.
NIV.5: There was a plot afoot among the Gentiles and Jews, together with their leaders, to mistreat them and stone them.
NIV.6: But they found out about it and fled to the Lycaonian cities of Lystra and Derbe and to the surrounding country,
NIV.7: where they continued to preach the good news.
NIV.8:
In Lystra and Derbe
In Lystra there sat a man crippled in his feet, who was lame from birth and had never walked.
NIV.9: He listened to Paul as he was speaking. Paul looked directly at him, saw that he had faith to be healed
NIV.10: and called out, “Stand up on your feet!” At that, the man jumped up and began to walk.
NIV.11: When the crowd saw what Paul had done, they shouted in the Lycaonian language, “The gods have come down to us in human form!”
NIV.12: Barnabas they called Zeus, and Paul they called Hermes because he was the chief speaker.
NIV.13: The priest of Zeus, whose temple was just outside the city, brought bulls and wreaths to the city gates because he and the crowd wanted to offer sacrifices to them.
NIV.14: But when the apostles Barnabas and Paul heard of this, they tore their clothes and rushed out into the crowd, shouting:
NIV.15: “Men, why are you doing this? We too are only men, human like you. We are bringing you good news, telling you to turn from these worthless things to the living God, who made heaven and earth and sea and everything in them.
NIV.16: In the past, he let all nations go their own way.
NIV.17: Yet he has not left himself without testimony: He has shown kindness by giving you rain from heaven and crops in their seasons; he provides you with plenty of food and fills your hearts with joy.”
NIV.18: Even with these words, they had difficulty keeping the crowd from sacrificing to them.
NIV.19: Then some Jews came from Antioch and Iconium and won the crowd over. They stoned Paul and dragged him outside the city, thinking he was dead.
NIV.20: But after the disciples had gathered around him, he got up and went back into the city. The next day he and Barnabas left for Derbe.
NIV.21:
The Return to Antioch in Syria
They preached the good news in that city and won a large number of disciples. Then they returned to Lystra, Iconium and Antioch,
NIV.22: strengthening the disciples and encouraging them to remain true to the faith. “We must go through many hardships to enter the kingdom of God,” they said.
NIV.23: Paul and Barnabas appointed elders for them in each church and, with prayer and fasting, committed them to the Lord, in whom they had put their trust.
NIV.24: After going through Pisidia, they came into Pamphylia,
NIV.25: and when they had preached the word in Perga, they went down to Attalia.
NIV.26: From Attalia they sailed back to Antioch, where they had been committed to the grace of God for the work they had now completed.
NIV.27: On arriving there, they gathered the church together and reported all that God had done through them and how he had opened the door of faith to the Gentiles.
NIV.28: And they stayed there a long time with the disciples.

NRSVCE.1: The same thing occurred in Iconium, where Paul and Barnabas went into the Jewish synagogue and spoke in such a way that a great number of both Jews and Greeks became believers.
NRSVCE.2: But the unbelieving Jews stirred up the Gentiles and poisoned their minds against the brothers.
NRSVCE.3: So they remained for a long time, speaking boldly for the Lord, who testified to the word of his grace by granting signs and wonders to be done through them.
NRSVCE.4: But the residents of the city were divided; some sided with the Jews, and some with the apostles.
NRSVCE.5: And when an attempt was made by both Gentiles and Jews, with their rulers, to mistreat them and to stone them,
NRSVCE.6: the apostles learned of it and fled to Lystra and Derbe, cities of Lycaonia, and to the surrounding country;
NRSVCE.7: and there they continued proclaiming the good news.
NRSVCE.8: In Lystra there was a man sitting who could not use his feet and had never walked, for he had been crippled from birth.
NRSVCE.9: He listened to Paul as he was speaking. And Paul, looking at him intently and seeing that he had faith to be healed,
NRSVCE.10: said in a loud voice, “Stand upright on your feet.” And the man sprang up and began to walk.
NRSVCE.11: When the crowds saw what Paul had done, they shouted in the Lycaonian language, “The gods have come down to us in human form!”
NRSVCE.12: Barnabas they called Zeus, and Paul they called Hermes, because he was the chief speaker.
NRSVCE.13: The priest of Zeus, whose temple was just outside the city, brought oxen and garlands to the gates; he and the crowds wanted to offer sacrifice.
NRSVCE.14: When the apostles Barnabas and Paul heard of it, they tore their clothes and rushed out into the crowd, shouting,
NRSVCE.15: “Friends, why are you doing this? We are mortals just like you, and we bring you good news, that you should turn from these worthless things to the living God, who made the heaven and the earth and the sea and all that is in them.
NRSVCE.16: In past generations he allowed all the nations to follow their own ways;
NRSVCE.17: yet he has not left himself without a witness in doing good — giving you rains from heaven and fruitful seasons, and filling you with food and your hearts with joy.”
NRSVCE.18: Even with these words, they scarcely restrained the crowds from offering sacrifice to them.
NRSVCE.19: But Jews came there from Antioch and Iconium and won over the crowds. Then they stoned Paul and dragged him out of the city, supposing that he was dead.
NRSVCE.20: But when the disciples surrounded him, he got up and went into the city. The next day he went on with Barnabas to Derbe.
NRSVCE.21: After they had proclaimed the good news to that city and had made many disciples, they returned to Lystra, then on to Iconium and Antioch.
NRSVCE.22: There they strengthened the souls of the disciples and encouraged them to continue in the faith, saying, “It is through many persecutions that we must enter the kingdom of God.”
NRSVCE.23: And after they had appointed elders for them in each church, with prayer and fasting they entrusted them to the Lord in whom they had come to believe.
NRSVCE.24: Then they passed through Pisidia and came to Pamphylia.
NRSVCE.25: When they had spoken the word in Perga, they went down to Attalia.
NRSVCE.26: From there they sailed back to Antioch, where they had been commended to the grace of God for the work that they had completed.
NRSVCE.27: When they arrived, they called the church together and related all that God had done with them, and how he had opened a door of faith for the Gentiles.
NRSVCE.28: And they stayed there with the disciples for some time.

NET.1: The same thing happened in Iconium when Paul and Barnabas went into the Jewish synagogue and spoke in such a way that a large group of both Jews and Greeks believed.
NET.2: But the Jews who refused to believe stirred up the Gentiles and poisoned their minds against the brothers.
NET.3: So they stayed there for a considerable time, speaking out courageously for the Lord, who testified to the message of his grace, granting miraculous signs and wonders to be performed through their hands.
NET.4: But the population of the city was divided; some sided with the Jews, and some with the apostles.
NET.5: When both the Gentiles and the Jews (together with their rulers) made an attempt to mistreat them and stone them,
NET.6: Paul and Barnabas learned about it and fled to the Lycaonian cities of Lystra and Derbe and the surrounding region.
NET.7: There they continued to proclaim the good news.
NET.8: In Lystra sat a man who could not use his feet, lame from birth, who had never walked.
NET.9: This man was listening to Paul as he was speaking. When Paul stared intently at him and saw he had faith to be healed,
NET.10: he said with a loud voice, “Stand upright on your feet.” And the man leaped up and began walking.
NET.11: So when the crowds saw what Paul had done, they shouted in the Lycaonian language, “The gods have come down to us in human form!”
NET.12: They began to call Barnabas Zeus and Paul Hermes, because he was the chief speaker.
NET.13: The priest of the temple of Zeus, located just outside the city, brought bulls and garlands to the city gates; he and the crowds wanted to offer sacrifices to them.
NET.14: But when the apostles Barnabas and Paul heard about it, they tore their clothes and rushed out into the crowd, shouting,
NET.15: “Men, why are you doing these things? We too are men, with human natures just like you! We are proclaiming the good news to you, so that you should turn from these worthless things to the living God, who made the heaven, the earth, the sea, and everything that is in them.
NET.16: In past generations he allowed all the nations to go their own ways,
NET.17: yet he did not leave himself without a witness by doing good, by giving you rain from heaven and fruitful seasons, satisfying you with food and your hearts with joy.”
NET.18: Even by saying these things, they scarcely persuaded the crowds not to offer sacrifice to them.
NET.19: But Jews came from Antioch and Iconium, and after winning the crowds over, they stoned Paul and dragged him out of the city, presuming him to be dead.
NET.20: But after the disciples had surrounded him, he got up and went back into the city. On the next day he left with Barnabas for Derbe.
NET.21: After they had proclaimed the good news in that city and made many disciples, they returned to Lystra, to Iconium, and to Antioch.
NET.22: They strengthened the souls of the disciples and encouraged them to continue in the faith, saying, “We must enter the kingdom of God through many persecutions.”
NET.23: When they had appointed elders for them in the various churches, with prayer and fasting they entrusted them to the protection of the Lord in whom they had believed.
NET.24: Then they passed through Pisidia and came into Pamphylia,
NET.25: and when they had spoken the word in Perga, they went down to Attalia.
NET.26: From there they sailed back to Antioch, where they had been commended to the grace of God for the work they had now completed.
NET.27: When they arrived and gathered the church together, they reported all the things God had done with them, and that he had opened a door of faith for the Gentiles.
NET.28: So they spent considerable time with the disciples.
